title: Using Arrays in Functions
description: Learn how to pass an array and its size into a function, why changes made inside the function affect the original array, and how to rewrite sum/average as reusable functions.
congratsText: Great work! You can now write functions that operate on arrays.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Pass an array and its size into a function
  • Explain why the original array changes when a function modifies it
  • Rewrite sum/average logic as standalone, reusable functions
  • Recognize the standard signature used for array functions

sections.title: Passing an Array to a Function

quiz.question: How does a function's parameter list declare that it will receive an array?
options: By writing empty brackets after the parameter name, like int arr[]
By writing the word array before the parameter type
Arrays cannot be passed to functions in C at all
By listing every individual element as a separate parameter

sections.lesson:
Passing an Array to a Function
A function receives an array by declaring a parameter with empty brackets, like int arr[]. The size is almost always passed as a second parameter, since the function has no other way to know how long the array is.
void printArray(int arr[], int size) { for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { printf("%d ", arr[i]); } } int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; printArray(scores, 5);

sections.title: Why the Size Must Be Passed Separately

quiz.question: Why does an array function need size as its own parameter, unlike passing a single int?
options: A function cannot determine an array's length just by receiving it
C automatically adds a size parameter to every array function
The size parameter is optional and rarely actually used
Arrays store their own length as a hidden built-in value

sections.lesson:
Why the Size Must Be Passed Separately
Unlike some languages, a C array does not carry its length with it. Once inside a function, arr[] is really just an address — the function has no way to know where the data ends unless told explicitly.
int sumArray(int arr[], int size) { // size tells the loop exactly when to stop int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) sum += arr[i]; return sum; }

sections.title: Arrays Are Passed by Reference

quiz.question: What happens to the original array in main() if a function modifies arr[0] inside its body?
options: 10 is printed — the original array itself was changed
5 is printed — arrays are always copied before being passed
The program fails to compile
0 is printed — arr[0] resets once the function ends

sections.lesson:
Arrays Are Passed by Reference
Unlike a plain int or float, passing an array to a function does not copy its contents — it passes the array's address. That means changes made inside the function are changes to the original array, not a local copy.
void doubleFirst(int arr[]) { arr[0] = arr[0] * 2; // modifies the real array } int nums[3] = {5, 10, 15}; doubleFirst(nums); // nums[0] is now 10, not 5

sections.title: Contrast: Single Variables Are Passed by Value

quiz.question: How does passing a plain int differ from passing an array?
options: 5 is printed — x is a local copy, so changing it doesn't affect y
100 is printed — int variables also change the original
The program fails to compile because x and y have different names
0 is printed — uninitialized copies default to zero

sections.lesson:
Contrast: Single Variables Are Passed by Value
A plain int, float, or char is copied when passed to a function — changes inside the function stay inside the function. This is the opposite of how arrays behave, which is exactly why array functions can update data permanently while single-variable functions cannot.

sections.title: Rewriting sumArray() as a Standalone Function

quiz.question: What does sumArray(scores, 5) return for int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88};?

sections.lesson:
Rewriting sumArray() as a Standalone Function
Week 4's inline sum loop becomes a reusable function with the standard array-function shape: array, size, then a return value.
int sumArray(int arr[], int size) { int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { sum += arr[i]; } return sum; } int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; int total = sumArray(scores, 5); // 433

sections.title: Rewriting averageArray() Using sumArray()

quiz.question: Why does averageArray() call sumArray() instead of writing its own separate summing loop?
options: It reuses logic that already exists, instead of duplicating the same loop
C requires every float function to call an int function first
sumArray() runs the loop faster than a new loop would
It is required syntax — averageArray() cannot compute alone

sections.lesson:
Rewriting averageArray() Using sumArray()
Once sumArray() exists, averageArray() doesn't need its own loop — it just calls sumArray() and divides. This is functions calling functions, applied to real array work.
float averageArray(int arr[], int size) { return (float) sumArray(arr, size) / size; } float avg = averageArray(scores, 5); // 86.6

sections.title: Complete Example: A Small Array Toolkit

quiz.question: In this toolkit, why does countAbove() also take a threshold parameter in addition to arr and size?
options: The comparison value changes depending on how the function is called, so it must be passed in
C requires exactly three parameters for any array function
threshold secretly represents the array's size a second time
Without it, the function would not compile at all

sections.lesson:
Complete Example: A Small Array Toolkit
A handful of small, focused functions can now work together on the same array.
int sumArray(int arr[], int size) { ... } float averageArray(int arr[], int size) { ... } int countAbove(int arr[], int size, int threshold) { int count = 0; for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) { if (arr[i] > threshold) count++; } return count; } int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; int total = sumArray(scores, 5); float avg = averageArray(scores, 5); int aboveAvg = countAbove(scores, 5, (int) avg);

sections.title: Best Practice: Keep the Signature Consistent

quiz.question: Why is it helpful for every array function in a program to follow the same arr, size parameter order?
options: It makes the functions predictable and easy to combine without confusion
C refuses to compile array functions with inconsistent parameter order
It has no practical benefit beyond looking neat
It automatically makes every function run in constant time

sections.lesson:
Best Practice: Keep the Signature Consistent
Once every array function follows the same arr, size (and optional extra parameters after) order, they become easy to read, easy to call together, and easy to extend — a habit that pays off heavily once search and sort functions are added.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: What is the key takeaway about arrays and functions from this discussion?
options: Arrays are passed by reference, so functions can read AND permanently modify the original data
Arrays must be fully copied every time they're passed into a function
Only void functions are allowed to accept an array parameter
The size of an array never needs to be passed to a function

sections.lesson:
Recap
Arrays pass by reference — a function receiving arr[] works with the original data, not a copy, which is why array functions can genuinely modify what they're given. Pairing the array with an explicit size parameter is the standard shape you'll reuse for every search, sort, and matrix function from here on.
int sumArray(int arr[], int size) { int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < size; i++) sum += arr[i]; return sum; }
